title: Linus Torvalds to critics of AI coding in Linux: "Fork it. Or just walk away."
gist: Linux creator Linus Torvalds publicly backed the use of AI coding and review tools in the Linux kernel, telling objectors to fork the project or leave, in a debate sparked by the AI review tool Sashiko (53.6% true-positive bug detection, ~20% false-positive rate) — even as a widely cited METR study found AI-using open source developers were actually 19% less productive despite feeling 20% more productive.

themes.theme: AI coding tools reach core open-source infrastructure

insights.claim: Linus Torvalds declared he is 'willing to absolutely put my foot down' in support of AI tool use in the Linux kernel and told critics who object to LLM-generated code that they can fork the project or walk away, rejecting the Software Freedom Conservancy's call to support developers who reject LLM-gen-AI.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: The maintainer of the world's most critical open-source project has taken an unambiguous pro-AI-tooling stance, which will likely accelerate AI-assisted development norms across the broader open-source ecosystem that depends on Linux governance precedent.
insights.quote: Linux is not one of those anti-AI projects, and if somebody has issues with that, they can do the open-source thing and fork it. Or just walk away.
insights.locator: opening paragraphs, Linux kernel mailing list post

insights.claim: Sashiko, an agentic Linux kernel code review system at the center of the debate, independently found 53.6% of the bugs that were later fixed by human coders in subsequent commits, but its maintainers estimate its false-positive rate at close to 20%.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete efficacy benchmark for AI-based code review tools in a demanding real-world codebase (~54% recall, ~20% false-positive rate) that can be used to calibrate expectations for similar AI code-review products entering enterprise software workflows.

insights.claim: Torvalds argued AI coding tool utility is 'no longer in question' as of mid-2026, a claim he says wasn't clearly true even a year earlier, framing his support as based on technical merit rather than hype.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A credible, technically skeptical, non-vendor voice endorsing AI coding tool maturity is a stronger signal for AI-coding-tool adoption curves than typical vendor marketing claims.
insights.quote: AI is a tool, just like other tools we use. And it's clearly a useful one. It may not have been that 'clearly' even just a year ago, but it's no longer in question today.
insights.locator: It's just useful… or is it? section

insights.claim: A METR study found that open source developers using AI coding tools were actually 19 percent less productive than those who didn't use them, even though the same AI-using developers felt 20 percent more productive — a gap between perceived and measured productivity.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A rigorous empirical counterpoint to AI-coding-productivity hype that investors in AI developer-tools should weigh heavily — perceived productivity gains from AI coding assistants may not translate into measured output, at least as of the original 2025 study.

insights.claim: In a February 2026 follow-up update, the same METR researchers said they believe developers are now more sped up by AI tools than their original 2025 estimates suggested, based on early raw results and participant conversations.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Suggests the AI-coding productivity picture may be improving quickly, but the researchers themselves flag this as early/raw data, so it should be treated as a tentative trend rather than confirmed reversal of the original negative finding.
insights.quote: we believe it is likely that developers are more sped up from AI tools now—in early 2026—compared to our estimates from early 2025

insights.claim: Not all open-source developers accept AI tooling: in May, the maintainer of the jqwik Java testing library planted a hidden malicious prompt-injection instruction designed to make vibe-coding bots delete all of the project's tests and code.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Illustrates an emerging adversarial risk for AI coding agents operating on open-source repositories — prompt-injection sabotage — that AI coding tool vendors and enterprise adopters need defenses against.
